difficulty_factors: Wool IS a protein fiber — protease enzymes damage wool by digesting keratin
Wool felts with heat, agitation, and alkaline conditions — all commonly used in stain removal
Narrow pH tolerance (5.5–8.0) limits available chemistry
Chlorine bleach is NEVER safe on wool — causes irreversible yellowing
Combination stain requires protein → tannin → oil sequencing

diy.title: Coffee with Cream on Wool
ingredients: Mild dish soap (1 drop in 1 cup cool water)
mixSteps: Do NOT use enzyme cleaners with protease on wool — wool IS protein and protease damages the fiber
Do NOT use ammonia on wool — alkali opens the cuticle scales causing felting and yellowing
Mix 1 drop mild dish soap into 1 cup cool water for protein/fat step
Have white vinegar ready undiluted for tannin step

steps.instruction: Blot immediately with a clean white cloth dampened with COLD water. Gently press and lift — never rub or scrub wool. Work from outside the stain inward.
steps.why: Cream protein sets with heat. Wool's cuticle scales open when wet and agitated — rubbing causes irreversible felting. Cold blotting is the only safe mechanical action on wet wool.

steps.instruction: Apply the dilute dish soap solution to a clean cloth and gently dab the stain. Let sit 3–5 minutes. Blot with a fresh cloth dampened with cool water.
steps.why: The surfactant emulsifies milk fat and loosens casein protein without enzymes (unsafe on wool) or ammonia (causes felting). Dish soap at this dilution is near-neutral pH — within wool's safe range (5.5–8.0).

steps.instruction: Mix white vinegar 1:2 with cool water (one-third vinegar, two-thirds water). Dab onto the stain. Let sit 2–3 minutes. Blot with cool water.
steps.why: Dilute acetic acid breaks hydrogen bonds between coffee tannin and wool fiber. Vinegar must be diluted more for wool than cotton — wool tolerates a narrower acid range. The slightly acidic pH (around 4–5) is actually beneficial for wool, as it closes the cuticle scales.

steps.instruction: If brown color remains, test 3% hydrogen peroxide on a hidden seam. If safe, dab sparingly and leave 3–5 minutes maximum. Blot with cool water.
steps.why: Peroxide is a limited option on wool — 3% max concentration, cool water only, brief contact. It oxidizes the tannin chromophore but can yellow wool with extended exposure. On wool, peroxide without ammonia — the ammonia catalyst is too alkaline for wool.

steps.instruction: Lay flat on a clean towel to dry. Gently reshape if needed. Do NOT hang, wring, tumble dry, or apply heat until stain is confirmed gone.
steps.why: Wool shrinks and distorts with heat and gravity when wet. Flat drying preserves shape. Heat sets any residual tannin permanently.

diy.ifStillThere: Take to a professional dry cleaner — specify that it's coffee with cream on wool. A cleaner using hydrocarbon solvent (preferred for wool) can safely spot with professional tannin formula at controlled dilution. Do not attempt chlorine bleach — it will destroy wool fiber.
proTips: NEVER use enzyme cleaners with protease on wool — they digest the keratin protein that IS the wool fiber.
NEVER use ammonia on wool — alkaline pH opens cuticle scales, causing felting and permanent texture change.
NEVER use chlorine bleach on wool — causes irreversible yellowing and fiber destruction.
Wool is most safely cleaned by dry cleaning — GreenEarth or hydrocarbon solvents are A-rated for wool safety.
diy.safety: Test ALL solutions on a hidden seam — wool dyes are often acid dyes that can bleed with acid treatment. If you see color transfer, stop and take to a professional. No chlorine bleach, no ammonia, no enzyme cleaners on wool.
diy.whyThisWorks: Coffee with cream on wool requires a modified combination approach: surfactant for protein/fat (since enzymes are unsafe), dilute acid for tannin (within wool's pH range), and very limited oxidation for residual color. Wool's protein fiber structure severely limits the chemistry arsenal — what works on cotton can destroy wool.

home.title: Coffee with Cream on Wool — Careful Removal
home.confidenceNote: Wool limits available chemistry significantly. Fresh stains (under 30 minutes) have good odds with surfactant + vinegar. Aged stains often require professional dry cleaning.

primaryProduct.name: Carbona Stain Devils #1 (Coffee, Tea, Wine)
primaryProduct.why: Formulated for tannin stains. Test on wool first — milder than professional tannin formula.
primaryProduct.amazonSearchQuery: Carbona Stain Devils 1 coffee tea wine

alternativeProducts.name: Dawn Ultra Dishwashing Liquid (Original)
alternativeProducts.why: Gentle surfactant for protein/fat phase — use extremely dilute (1 drop per cup water).

home.prepStep: Blot immediately with cold water. NEVER rub wool — causes felting. NEVER use hot water.

method.instruction: Blot with clean white cloth and cold water. Press and lift — never rub or scrub.
method.why: Wet wool is extremely susceptible to felting from mechanical action.

method.instruction: Dab dilute dish soap solution (1 drop per cup cold water) onto stain. Let sit 3–5 minutes. Blot with cold water.
method.why: Surfactant emulsifies cream fat and loosens protein without the enzymes or alkali that would damage wool.

method.instruction: Dab dilute white vinegar (1 part vinegar to 2 parts water) onto stain. Let sit 2–3 minutes. Blot with cold water.
method.why: Dilute acid breaks tannin bonds. Slightly acidic pH actually helps wool — closes cuticle scales.

method.instruction: Lay flat on clean towel to dry. Inspect before any heat or pressing.
method.why: Flat drying prevents distortion. Heat sets residual tannin.

materialWarnings.warning: NEVER use enzyme cleaners (protease) — wool IS protein and enzymes will damage the fiber.
NEVER use ammonia — alkaline pH opens cuticle scales, causing irreversible felting.
NEVER use chlorine bleach — destroys wool protein and causes permanent yellowing.
NEVER use sodium percarbonate (OxiClean) — alkaline pH + oxygen release damages wool protein.
Temperature limit: 30°C / 86°F. Exceeding causes felting and shrinkage.

ifItDoesntWork.escalation: Take to a professional dry cleaner who uses hydrocarbon or GreenEarth solvent. Explain it's coffee with cream on wool and what you've tried.

proTips: Speed is critical on wool — the limited chemistry means you rely more on flushing fresh stain than chemical power.
Wool is best dry cleaned — if the garment is valuable, skip home treatment and go straight to a professional.
After treatment, a vinegar rinse (dilute) actually conditions wool and closes the cuticle.
home.whyThisWorks: Wool's protein structure severely limits the chemistry available for stain removal. The approach uses only wool-safe agents: neutral surfactant for protein/fat, dilute acid for tannin, and avoids the enzymes, alkali, and bleach that would damage the fiber.
home.timeEstimate: 25–45 min (professional dry cleaning recommended for aged stains)

pro.title: Coffee with Cream on Wool — Professional Combination Protocol
pro.stainChemistry: Classification: Combination (protein + tannin + oil). Same three-component stain as cotton, but wool's protein fiber structure eliminates most of the professional arsenal. Wool is keratin protein — protease enzyme digests it. Wool cuticle scales open at alkaline pH and with heat — causing felting. pH tolerance 5.5–8.0 (narrow). Heat max 30°C wet. Chlorine bleach: NEVER. Sodium percarbonate: NEVER. Ammonia: test carefully, use only if essential and briefly. The Dan Eisen combination procedure calls for ammonia — on wool, substitute with very mild protein formula tested first, or skip the alkaline step entirely and rely on surfactant for the protein phase.

spottingProtocol.technique: Water gun at safe distance — no steam under any circumstances
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cold water gun only. NEVER steam on wool with this stain — steam sets casein protein permanently AND opens wool cuticle scales causing felting. Flush gently at 6+ inch distance. Cold water removes bulk of fresh stain.

spottingProtocol.technique: Lubricate and tamp gently — dissolve protein and emulsify fat without pH extremes
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply neutral lubricant. Use nonionic surfactant — anionic surfactants have high pH that damages wool. Tamp VERY gently — wool felts with mechanical action when wet. The neutral lubricant emulsifies milk fat and loosens casein protein within wool's safe pH range. Do NOT use protein formula (contains ammonia/alkali) as primary approach — test first if needed.

spottingProtocol.technique: Clear protein/fat residue before tannin treatment
spottingProtocol.instruction: Thorough cold water flush. Must clear all surfactant and loosened protein before applying tannin formula.

spottingProtocol.agent: Tannin formula (diluted for wool — check pH)
spottingProtocol.technique: Acidic tannin spotter — break tannin-wool hydrogen bonds
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply tannin formula — test on wool first. Dilute if pH is below 5.5 (wool's lower safe limit). Tamp very gently. The acetic acid breaks hydrogen bonds between coffee tannin and wool keratin. The slightly acidic pH is actually within wool's comfort zone and helps close cuticle scales. Flush with cold water after 2–3 minutes. Do NOT follow with protein formula to neutralize acid on wool — the slightly acidic residue is actually beneficial for wool fiber.

spottingProtocol.agent: H₂O₂ 3% only — no ammonia catalyst on wool
spottingProtocol.technique: Very limited oxidation for residual tannin color
spottingProtocol.instruction: If brown discoloration persists. Test H₂O₂ 3% on inconspicuous area first — wool can yellow with peroxide. Use 3% ONLY, cool application, no ammonia catalyst (ammonia is alkaline and opens cuticle). Brief contact — 3–5 minutes max. Flush with cold water. If wool shows any yellowing or texture change, stop immediately and flush.

spottingProtocol.agent: Cold water flush + dilute acetic acid rinse
spottingProtocol.technique: Final rinse at slightly acidic pH — close wool cuticle
spottingProtocol.instruction: Final cold water flush, followed by dilute acetic acid rinse (white vinegar 1:4 with water). This closes the wool cuticle scales, restores the fiber's natural slightly acidic surface, and prevents post-treatment felting during drying.

pro.escalation: If stain persists: dry clean in hydrocarbon solvent (A-rated for wool safety) or GreenEarth. Avoid PERC on wool if possible — residual moisture during PERC processing can cause felting. For stubborn tannin residue: oxalic acid is effective on tannin but LIMITED use on wool (D-rating, brief contact, 5% max concentration, test first). Reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite) is a last resort on wool — D-rating, 1g/kg max, very short contact, professional use only.
pro.customerExplanation: Coffee with cream on wool requires extra care because wool is a protein fiber — many standard stain-removal chemicals that work on cotton would damage wool. We used a gentle approach: mild surfactant for the cream, dilute acid for the coffee tannin, and limited oxidation for any remaining color. The treatment was kept within the narrow range of chemistry that wool tolerates safely.
commonMistakes: Using enzyme cleaner on wool — protease digests wool keratin, weakening the fiber permanently
Using ammonia or alkaline protein formula — opens wool cuticle scales, causes felting and can set tannin
Using steam or hot water — heat + wet wool = felting. Coffee protein sets permanently with heat.
Using chlorine bleach — irreversible yellowing and structural damage to wool protein
Using OxiClean (sodium percarbonate) — alkaline pH + oxygen generation damages wool
Scrubbing or aggressive tamping — wet wool felts with mechanical action
pro.neutralization: After treatment: dilute acetic acid rinse (vinegar 1:4) to close cuticle scales. Cold water final flush. Lay flat to dry — never hang wet wool.
pro.fabricRisks: Wool: protein fiber (keratin). pH 5.5–8.0 safe. Heat max 30°C wet. Felts with alkaline pH + heat + mechanical action. NEVER: protease enzyme, chlorine bleach, sodium percarbonate, strong alkali. Safe solvents: hydrocarbon (A), GreenEarth (A), Solvon K4 (A), CO₂ (A). PERC: caution (C) — residual moisture risk.
